title: Establishing a Career Executive Program Within the Civil Service System

flags.justification: Implements the Career Executive Committee's mandate (EO 10724, revoked by Sec. 6 of this order) by establishing an operational Career Executive Board; final authority to designate career executives remains with the President (Sec. 4: an employee becomes a participant 'only by being designated as a career executive by the President'), so the Board's role is nomination/recommendation, not final decision.
Sec. 5(c) requires the Board to submit an annual report of its activities to the President through the Commission — a built-in transparency mechanism for the Board's own operation.
A single, coherent personnel-management program; functions (identification, selection, placement, retention, training, classification recommendations) all serve one merit-program purpose.

Sec. 1(a)'s definition of 'career executive service' depends on positions being 'designated by the heads of the executive agencies concerned with the approval of the...Board' from among specified grades — a workable but not fully criteria-bound classification process, and Sec. 3(b)(1) nominations require the Board's own 'concurring' judgment without further-specified standards.
No incentive structure beyond the merit-based program itself; Sec. 2 caps political-party representation on the Board ('not more than three...shall be adherents of the same political party').
A standing personnel-management program; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No oversight channel narrowed; final designation authority remains with the President, and the Board reports annually through the Commission.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects; establishes the operational counterpart to a program whose study phase (EO 10724) was itself scored as a clean positive example.
No inter-agency conflict; the Board coordinates with, rather than duplicates, the Civil Service Commission's regulatory role (Sec. 5(b)).
No exemption architecture.

notes: Diff-aware, direct continuation: this order fulfills the mandate of EO 10724 ('Establishing a Career Executive Committee,' coded earlier in this batch as a clean positive example with a fixed Nov. 1, 1957 reporting deadline) and formally revokes it (Sec. 6). The operational program preserves the predecessor's positive features — explicit merit-system guardrails, political-party balance requirements, and retention of final decision authority with the President — rather than weakening them once the program moved from study phase to implementation.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10530, Providing for the Performance of Certain Functions Vested in or Subject to Approval of the President

flags.justification: A narrow update to a single delegated function (travel/transportation-expense regulation authority) within the broader EO 10530 framework, updating a statutory cross-reference to reflect a February 1958 amendment to the underlying Act.
No accountability mechanism removed by this specific amendment; it updates a citation, not the delegation's scope or check structure.
A single, narrow function update within the larger framework order.
The delegated authority (travel/transportation/household-goods expense regulations) is tied to specific statutory citations.
No incentive structure at issue.
A standing delegation-framework amendment; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.

This specific amendment's text does not itself contain approval-bypass language (it updates a statutory cross-reference for an existing delegated function); no oversight channel is narrowed by this particular change.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects from this narrow update.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture created by this specific amendment.

notes: Structural observation for the notable-patterns-log: EO 10530's own title, 'Providing for the Performance of Certain Functions Vested in or Subject to Approval of the President,' strongly suggests it is the central 'hub' framework order to which many of this batch's ministerial-approval-removal template instances (which typically take the form of amendments authorizing specific functions to be performed 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President') are likely anchored, rather than each instance being an independent, freestanding order. This specific amendment (EO 10759) does not itself use that bypass language — it is a routine cross-reference update — but its existence as 'Amendment of Executive Order No. 10530' confirms the hub order's ongoing role as the vehicle for adding new delegated functions over time. Not independently verified against each prior ministerial-approval-removal instance in this pass; flagged as worth checking whether EO 10536, 10583(b), 10592, 10593, or 10609 are themselves amendments to EO 10530 specifically.

title: Government Purchases of Crude Petroleum and Petroleum Products

flags.justification: Directs application of the Buy American Act to petroleum purchases based on the President's own determination that 'essential national security interests are adversely affected' by non-compliant imports and that such purchases 'would not be in the public interest' — self-determined findings with no defined review.
The determination follows receipt of advice from the Special Committee to Investigate Crude Oil Imports, an at-least-nominal outside input into the President's finding.
A single, coherent economic-policy mechanism (Buy American Act application to petroleum) is established.
'Essential national security interests are adversely affected' and 'not in the public interest' are undefined, self-judged standards with no criteria specified for what would satisfy or fail them.

Sec. 4 permits a supplier's own 'certification or representation of compliance' with the Voluntary Oil Import Program to be treated as 'presumptive evidence of compliance' in the absence of an official certificate — a self-certification mechanism that creates an incentive to self-attest compliance with limited independent verification.
A standing procurement-policy directive; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply, though Sec. 6 delays applicability thirty days to allow contract adjustment.
No court or congressional oversight channel is narrowed.

Creates a 'complying petroleum product' category tied to a supplier's participation history in the Voluntary Oil Import Program, directly shaping which private firms benefit from favorable federal procurement treatment based on a program that is itself voluntary and self-reported.

Sec. 5 disapplies the general Buy American Act procedures order (EO 10582) specifically for petroleum, carving out an entire commodity category from the government's standard determination process and substituting a national-security rationale for what is substantively an economic/import-protection measure.
No inter-agency conflict; the Voluntary Oil Import Program Administrator's certification role is clearly bounded.

Sec. 5 exempts crude petroleum and petroleum products entirely from the government-wide Buy American Act procedures order (EO 10582), a carve-out for a single commodity from the otherwise-uniform determination process.

notes: Follows from a 'Special Committee to Investigate Crude Oil Imports' recommendation; the national-security framing for what is substantively an oil-import protection measure, paired with a self-certification compliance mechanism (Sec. 4) and a blanket carve-out from the general Buy American Act procedures order (Sec. 5), makes this one of the more structurally notable economic-policy orders in this chunk.

title: Delegating to the Secretary of Defense the Authority of the President To Order to Active Duty Members of the Reserve Components of the Armed Forces Who Are in Medical, Dental, or Allied Specialist Categories and To Prescribe Regulations Governing the Appointment, Reappointment, and Promotion of Such Members

flags.justification: Delegates to the Secretary of Defense the authority to order reserve medical/dental/allied-specialist personnel under age 35 to up to 24 months of active duty 'with or without his consent' — a compulsory recall power over a defined professional population.

This is a direct statutory delegation of an authority Congress itself created (1957 amendments to the Universal Military Training and Service Act); no accountability mechanism is removed by delegating it to SecDef rather than the President personally exercising it.
Bundles two related, statutorily-linked functions (compulsory activation authority and appointment/promotion regulation authority for the same personnel category) into one delegation.
Eligibility criteria are objective and statute-defined (specialist category, age under 35, prior active-duty service of less than one year).

No incentive structure beyond the compulsory-service framework itself, which is a matter of explicit statutory design (Congress created this recall authority specifically for medical/dental personnel shortages).
A standing delegation for an ongoing statutory recall authority; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply, though the maximum tour length (24 months) is itself capped by statute.
No oversight channel narrowed.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects beyond the compulsory-recall authority already captured under Flag 1.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Revokes EO 10478 (Aug. 1953) as amended by EO 10658; the 'with or without his consent' compulsory-recall language is itself a direct statutory delegation (1957 Act amendments), not an executive-branch-invented mechanism, which is why Flag 1 is scored PRESENT rather than CRITICAL despite the significant individual liberty interest at stake.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10422, as Amended, Prescribing Procedures for Making Available to the Secretary General of the United Nations Certain Information Concerning United States Citizens Employed or Being Considered for Employment on the Secretariat of the United Nations

flags.justification: Expands the list of file sources (FBI, CSC, military/naval intelligence, other Government investigative agencies, congressional committee files, schools, employers, references) that the Civil Service Commission must consult in background investigations of U.S. citizens on the UN Secretariat; does not itself concentrate decision-making power in any one office.

This amendment touches only the investigation-conducting and information-forwarding procedure (paragraphs 2-4 of Part I); it does not alter the hearing-rights structure (written notice, counsel, cross-examination) established elsewhere in EO 10422 as amended by EO 10459, which this order leaves untouched.
Bundles a substantial expansion of investigative file sources with a new automatic-referral rule (derogatory information triggers automatic FBI full field investigation) in a single amendment.

'Derogatory' information triggering automatic FBI referral is not itself defined in the amended text (it cross-references 'the standard set forth in Part II of this order,' which is not reproduced here and was not independently verified in this pass).
No incentive structure at issue.
A standing investigative-procedure framework; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply, though the amendment itself is prospective (effective July 1, 1958).
No oversight channel is narrowed; if anything, more investigative sources are added, and derogatory findings trigger a more thorough (not less thorough) FBI review.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects beyond the broadened investigative scope already captured under Flag 3.
No inter-agency conflict; the amendment clarifies a multi-agency investigative referral chain (State to CSC to FBI).

No exemption architecture, though short-term employees (90 days or less) are exempted from the expanded local/community-level investigation sources (subparagraphs (f)-(j)) — a narrow, bounded carve-out.

notes: Continues the international-organization loyalty-review chain first logged at EO 10459 (which re-codified hearing rights — written notice, counsel, cross-examination — for this same population just five weeks after EO 10450 curtailed the analogous domestic Loyalty Review Board). This amendment broadens the investigative-source list and adds an automatic-FBI-referral trigger for derogatory findings, but — as far as the amended paragraphs show — leaves the hearing-rights protections established by EO 10459 untouched; the two amendments to EO 10422 (10459 in 1953, 10763 here in 1958) move in different directions on different parts of the same order: 10459 strengthened the back-end hearing rights, while 10763 broadens the front-end investigative reach.

title: Suspension of the Eight-Hour Law as to Laborers and Mechanics Employed by the Civil Aeronautics Administration on Public Work Essential to the National Defense

flags.justification: Suspends a 66-year-old statutory workday-hour protection for CAA laborers/mechanics based on the President's own finding that 'an extraordinary emergency exists,' invoking Proclamation 2914's December 1950 national-emergency declaration as continuing legal predicate more than seven years after the Korean War armistice (July 1953) that occasioned it.

Overtime pay is preserved as a condition of the suspension ('time and one-half for all hours of work in excess of eight hours'), so the underlying protection is not eliminated, only its trigger-point relaxed.
A single, narrow suspension of one statutory provision for one agency's public-works laborers.

'Extraordinary emergency' is asserted as a finding ('I find that...an extraordinary emergency exists') with no criteria specified beyond the general recitation of air-navigation-system modernization needs.
No incentive-structure issue beyond the emergency-predicate question already captured under Flag 4 and Flag 6.

'This order shall continue in effect until terminated or revoked by the President' — no expiration date, review point, or sunset is specified; the order rests on a nearly eight-year-old national-emergency proclamation (Proclamation 2914, Dec. 1950) that is treated as still legally live rather than expired, without any acknowledgment in the text that the Korean War itself ended in 1953.
No court or congressional oversight channel is narrowed.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.

Demonstrates how a national-emergency proclamation, once issued, can continue to be invoked as live legal authority for new, unrelated domestic-labor suspensions years after the triggering conflict ended, with no textual requirement to reassess whether the predicate emergency still exists.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture beyond the hour-limit suspension itself, already captured under Flag 1.

notes: Extends the Proclamation 2914 open-ended-duration pattern (previously: EO 10465 flagged; EO 10546 a positive fix with a fixed calendar date; EO 10616 and EO 10632 reverting to open-ended form) to April 1958 — nearly five years after the Korean War armistice and well past the point where several other orders in this corpus have already wound down other wartime-emergency measures (see this batch's 'normalization of extraordinary wartime/emergency authority' pattern: EO 10577, 10585, 10608, 10628). This instance does not meet the strict Zombie Emergency Trap sub-flag definition (which requires an *acknowledged* lapsed authority) because the order does not acknowledge that Proclamation 2914's underlying war has ended — it simply treats the seven-plus-year-old proclamation as current, unexpired legal predicate without discussion. That silence is arguably the more concerning pattern: rather than acknowledging and defending the continued use of a stale emergency, the order simply proceeds as if no time has passed at all.

title: Regulations Governing the Award of Life-Saving Medals Under the Medals of Honor Act

flags.justification: Establishes a defined, multi-step evidentiary and review process (application, affidavits, ICC staff committee review, ICC recommendation, Secretary of Commerce approval) for civilian life-saving medal awards; no single office holds unreviewable discretion.
Sec. 9.4 requires a three-person committee review, an ICC recommendation, and a final decision by the Secretary of Commerce acting for the President — multiple sequential decision points.
A single, coherent honorary-award administration function.

Sec. 9.2 specifies detailed, fact-based evidentiary standards ('name of city, town, or village,' 'date, time of day, and condition of weather,' named witnesses) rather than a vague or self-judged standard.
No incentive structure at issue.
Sec. 9.7 imposes a clean two-year filing deadline from the date of the underlying incident.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency conflict; ICC and Secretary of Commerce roles are clearly sequenced.
No exemption architecture; Sec. 9.7's proviso excluding pre-June-13-1957 motor-vehicle incidents from the newly expanded coverage is a bounded transition rule, not an ongoing exemption.

notes: Supersedes 1926 regulations; a clean, well-specified administrative process with defined evidentiary standards and filing deadlines.

title: Delegating to the Director of the Bureau of the Budget the Authority of the President To Approve Regulations Relating to the Rental of Substandard Housing for Members of the Uniformed Services

flags.justification: Delegates a narrow but substantive regulatory-approval function (approving substandard-housing rental regulations for uniformed-service members) to the BoB Director.
No outside review mechanism is specified for the Director's approvals beyond the reporting requirement in Sec. 3.
A single, narrow regulatory-approval function.
The delegated authority tracks a specific statutory provision (section 407(b) of the Aug. 30, 1957 act) rather than an open-ended standard.
No incentive structure at issue.

The authority is expressly bounded to the life of section 407(b), and regulations may only be made effective 'between December 31, 1957, and the date on which section 407(b) ceases to be in force' — a clean statutory sunset tether.

Sec. 1's language — the Director is 'authorized and empowered, without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President, to exercise the authority vested in the President' — is a further instance of the ministerial-approval-removal template already tracked repeatedly in this batch (most recently EO 10729, EO 10747).
No third-party incentive structure beyond the underlying housing-rental subject matter.
No material second-order effects beyond the bypass mechanism already captured under Flag 7.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture beyond the delegation itself.

notes: Ministerial-approval-removal template, next instance in the series (following EO 10536, 10583(b), 10592, 10593, 10609, 10613, 10637, 10661, 10729, 10747). Sec. 3's reporting requirement (Secretaries must furnish the Director reports 'as he may require and at such times as he may specify') is at least a self-initiated monitoring mechanism, though the Director controls its scope and timing himself.

title: Further Extension of the Existence of the Quetico-Superior Committee

flags.justification: A routine extension of an existing advisory committee's charter; no new authority granted.
No accountability mechanism affected.
No bundling.
No enforcement standard at issue.
No incentive structure.
A clean, explicit four-year extension with a fixed end date (June 30, 1962) rather than an open-ended continuation.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency effect.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Positive example: a committee first created in 1934 has been extended 'from time to time' but each extension (including this one) is bounded to a specific fixed end date rather than being made permanent or open-ended.

title: Termination of the Airspace Reservation Established by Executive Order No. 7138 Over a Portion of the Aleutian Islands, Alaska

flags.justification: Terminates a restriction rather than creating or concentrating authority.
No accountability mechanism affected.
No bundling.
No enforcement standard at issue.
No incentive structure.
The order itself is the clean, final termination of a decades-old restriction — the outcome a sunset provision is meant to achieve.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency effect.
No exemption architecture.

notes: A deregulatory order terminating a 1935 airspace reservation in its entirety; a clean example of authority being wound down rather than accumulated.

title: Designating the International Hydrographic Bureau as a Public International Organization Entitled To Enjoy Certain Privileges, Exemptions, and Immunities

flags.justification: Routine designation under the International Organizations Immunities Act.
No accountability mechanism affected.
Single, narrow designation.
Tracks specific statutory and treaty authority.
No incentive structure at issue.
A standing status designation; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
The order expressly preserves rather than displaces any treaty/congressional privileges the organization may separately hold.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency conflict.
Grants standard privileges, exemptions, and immunities to a named international organization — a narrow, routine exemption architecture common to this template.

notes: Routine administrative designation, consistent with EO 10727's IAEA/UPU designation earlier in this batch.

title: Creating an Emergency Board To Investigate a Dispute Between American Airlines, Incorporated, and Certain of its Employees Represented by the Air Line Pilots Association, International

flags.justification: RLA Emergency Board positive template — three-member investigatory board with a fixed 30-day reporting deadline.
Fixed 30-day reporting requirement to the President.
Single, narrow fact-finding function.
Trigger standard is the National Mediation Board's statutory judgment, not open-ended presidential discretion.
Explicit conflict-of-interest bar on Board members.
Self-terminating: 30 days to report, then a further statutorily-fixed 30-day status-quo period.
Implements, rather than evades, the Railway Labor Act's statutory dispute-resolution structure.
No third-party incentive structure beyond the parties to the labor dispute.
No material second-order effects beyond ordinary RLA emergency-board procedure.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: RLA Emergency Board positive template; consistent with dozens of prior instances in this batch, always scoring 0%.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10534, Relating to the Supervision and Direction of the Saint Lawrence Seaway Development Corporation

flags.justification: Splits supervisory authority between the Secretary of Defense (construction phase only) and the Secretary of Commerce (ongoing operations), rather than concentrating it in one office.
No accountability mechanism removed; oversight of the Corporation continues to reside with named Cabinet officers.
A single, coherent supervisory-authority allocation.
Sec. 2(b) enumerates specific areas of Commerce's supervisory authority (general policies, seaway operation/maintenance, services and facilities, tolls) rather than an open-ended standard.
No incentive structure at issue.

Sec. 1 expressly provides that the Secretary of Defense's construction-phase authority 'shall terminate upon the completion of the construction so authorized' — a clean, self-executing sunset tied to an objective milestone.
No oversight channel narrowed.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects.

Sec. 4 affirmatively requires the two Secretaries to 'keep each other fully and currently informed' on matters affecting the other's responsibility — a built-in coordination mechanism specifically designed to prevent the kind of dual-authority friction that could otherwise arise from split supervision.
No exemption architecture.

notes: A clean positive example: the construction-phase/operations-phase split is paired with both an automatic sunset (Sec. 1) for the temporary authority and a mutual-information-sharing requirement (Sec. 4) to prevent inter-agency friction — addressing exactly the kind of split-authority risk flagged elsewhere in this batch as a potential source of inter-agency cannibalization.

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10673 Relating to the Fitness of American Youth

flags.justification: Establishes a Citizens Advisory Committee whose membership and chairman are designated at the discretion of the Council Chairman; a narrow structural amendment to an existing advisory body.
No accountability mechanism affected.
No bundling.
No criteria are specified for the Council Chairman's selection of Advisory Committee members.
No incentive structure at issue.
Standing advisory body; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Low-stakes advisory-committee structuring amendment; the undefined selection standard (Flag 4) carries minimal real-world weight given the subject matter.

title: Delegating and Transferring Certain Functions and Affairs to the Office of Defense and Civilian Mobilization

flags.justification: Implements Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1958 by merging the Office of Defense Mobilization and the Federal Civil Defense Administration into a single new 'Office of Defense and Civilian Mobilization,' redelegating to its Director every function previously assigned across at least 19 separate prior Executive orders (Sec. 4(b)) plus 7 more civil-defense-specific orders (Sec. 4(a)) — consolidating both wartime industrial-mobilization authority and domestic civil-defense authority under a single Director, with power of further redelegation (Sec. 2(a)).

Sec. 6 establishes a Defense and Civilian Mobilization Board chaired by the Director himself, composed of agency heads who serve only 'with their consent' — a voluntary, advisory-only body with no binding check on the Director's exercise of the consolidated authority.

A single order bundles the entirety of the pre-existing defense-mobilization apparatus (ODM) with the entirety of the pre-existing civil-defense apparatus (FCDA) under one office and one Director — two previously distinct missions (wartime industrial mobilization and domestic disaster/attack civil defense) now under unified control.

Sec. 9 authorizes the Director to 'issue such regulations as he may deem necessary or desirable to carry out the purposes of this order' — a broad, self-judged rulemaking standard with no further specification.
No distinct incentive structure beyond the consolidation itself.
A standing government-reorganization order; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply to an executive-branch restructuring.

Sec. 8 preserves, without fresh review, every regulation, ruling, order, determination, and agreement previously issued under the 26+ orders being consolidated — the reorganization carries forward the accumulated regulatory apparatus wholesale rather than subjecting any of it to renewed scrutiny at the moment of consolidation.
No private third-party incentive structure is implicated by this reorganization order itself.

This is the sixteenth data point in the ODM-Director-authority-accumulation narrative tracked throughout this batch (EO 10161→10193→10433→10438→10461→10480→10553→10574→10593→10601→10634→10638→10660→10662→10705→10773), and arguably its most significant consolidation event: EO 10480 (Aug. 1953) had already unified the defense-mobilization program under one Director; this order goes further by fusing that entire apparatus with the separate civil-defense chain of authority (FCDA), placing wartime industrial mobilization and domestic civil-defense/disaster-response authority under a single office for the first time.

The consolidation is explicitly designed to eliminate, not create, inter-agency overlap between ODM and FCDA by merging them into one body; Sec. 5 cleans up a cross-reference in EO 10737 (disaster relief) to match the new organizational structure.

Sec. 3's blanket cross-reference-updating mechanism includes a self-administered carve-out: 'Except in instances wherein the provisions concerned are for any reason inapplicable as of the effective date' — the Director (or the order itself) determines which of the 26+ referenced orders' provisions are 'for any reason inapplicable,' with no criteria specified for that determination.

notes: Major standalone finding, logged in notable-patterns-log.md: the sixteenth and most consequential link yet in the ODM-authority narrative. Implements Reorganization Plan No. 1 of 1958 to merge defense mobilization and civil defense into the new Office of Defense and Civilian Mobilization, consolidating 26+ prior orders' worth of delegated presidential authority under a single Director, with Sec. 8's continuity clause carrying forward all prior regulatory action without fresh review. Notable historical context: this reorganization is itself renamed just two months later by EO 10782 ('Office of Civil and Defense Mobilization'), suggesting the July 1958 name/structure was provisional pending further legislative action.

title: Providing for the Protection of the Civil-Service Rights of Federal Personnel Who Transfer to the International Atomic Energy Agency

flags.justification: Delegates a narrow, protective regulation-issuing function to the Civil Service Commission (rather than a single individual) to safeguard the civil-service rights of employees who transfer to the IAEA.

Sec. 3(e) provides a defined right of appeal to the Civil Service Commission for any employee denied re-employment, with the Commission's decision binding on the department or agency concerned ('shall take the action necessary to effectuate the decision') — a genuine, enforceable outside check.
A single, coherent civil-service-protection function.

Specific, objective protections are enumerated (retirement/insurance retention, compensation-rate entitlement, service credit, sick-leave restoration) with defined time windows (ninety days, three consecutive years).
No perverse incentive structure; the order protects, rather than incentivizes, a specific transfer decision.
A standing employee-protection framework tied to an ongoing international-agency-participation statute; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No oversight channel is narrowed; Sec. 3(e)'s appeal-to-CSC mechanism is an affirmative, enforceable check.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material adverse second-order effects; the order is designed to remove a disincentive (loss of civil-service rights) to public-service international-agency staffing.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture beyond the protective benefits themselves, which are extensions of existing rights rather than carve-outs from them.

notes: A clean positive example: Sec. 3(e)'s appeal-to-the-Commission mechanism, with a binding final decision that the losing agency 'shall take the action necessary to effectuate,' is one of the more concretely enforceable individual-appeal rights found in this batch — worth contrasting with the several gatekeeping/acceptance-determination provisions elsewhere in this batch that supply no defined appeal channel at all (e.g., EO 10728's fund-raising-agency acceptance determination).

title: Amendment of Executive Order No. 10633, Establishing an Airspace Reservation Over the Las Vegas Project, Las Vegas, Nevada

flags.justification: A technical enlargement of an airspace reservation to align with an AEC test-site boundary; no power concentration.
No accountability mechanism affected.
Single, narrow boundary amendment.
Boundaries are defined by precise latitude/longitude coordinates.
No incentive structure at issue.
A standing airspace-reservation designation; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency conflict; the amendment reallocates airspace previously restricted for Air Force bombing/gunnery use to the AEC reservation, a clean handoff.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Routine technical boundary amendment; low structural weight.

title: Delegating to the Secretary of Defense Certain Authority of the President Relating to the Modification of Standards and Requirements With Respect to the Induction of Persons Into the Armed, and For Other Purposes

flags.justification: A narrow statutory delegation of induction-standards rulemaking authority; redelegable only to Senate-confirmed Defense Department officials, a built-in accountability floor.
No accountability mechanism removed by this delegation.
Single, narrow delegation.
Tracks a specific statutory provision (the last proviso of section 4(a) of the Universal Military Training and Service Act, as just added by the same day's amendatory act).
No incentive structure at issue.
A standing delegation for an ongoing statutory induction-standards function; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No oversight channel narrowed.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: The redelegation limit to Senate-confirmed officials only is a positive accountability feature, consistent with other well-bounded delegations in this batch (e.g., EO 10705, EO 10741).

title: Amending Executive Order No. 10758 To Increase the Membership of the Career Executive Board

flags.justification: Expands the Career Executive Board from five to seven members, proportionally increasing both private-life and agency-representative seats; the underlying program structure and the President's final-designation authority (Sec. 4 of EO 10758) are unaffected.
No accountability mechanism removed; the annual-report-to-the-President requirement (Sec. 5(c) of EO 10758) is unaffected by this membership amendment.
Single, narrow membership amendment.
No new discretionary standard introduced.
No incentive structure altered; the political-party balance cap is proportionally preserved (not more than four of seven, up from not more than three of five).
Standing program; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply to a membership-size amendment.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Diff-aware, direct continuation: third order in the Career Executive Program chain (EO 10724 -> EO 10758 -> EO 10777). This capacity expansion proportionally preserves the political-party balance safeguard from EO 10758 rather than diluting it.

title: Authorizing the Appointment of William K. Harris to a Competitive Position Without Compliance With the Civil Service Act and Rules

flags.justification: A named-individual exception from the merit-based competitive civil-service hiring system, authorized by the President directly for one specific person.

No criteria, justification, or review mechanism is stated in the order itself beyond the bare recital that it 'is recommended by the Secretary of the Navy' — no explanation of why competitive hiring rules do not apply in this instance.
A single, narrow personnel action; not bundled with other authorities.

No standard whatsoever is given for why this named individual, specifically, is exempted from the Civil Service Act and Rules — unlike the categorical Schedule A/B/C exemption mechanisms tracked elsewhere in this batch, this is an individualized, criterion-free bypass naming one person by name.
No incentive structure beyond the individualized bypass already captured under Flag 4.
A one-time personnel action; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No court or congressional oversight channel is narrowed by this individual personnel action.
No private third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects beyond the individualized-exemption pattern already captured under Flag 4.
No inter-agency conflict.

This is the most direct and personal form of exemption architecture found in this batch: rather than creating a category (as Schedule A/B/C do) or a bounded procedural carve-out, the order exempts one named individual from the entire competitive civil-service hiring framework with no stated reason.

notes: A distinct type of exemption-architecture finding worth tracking separately from the Schedule A/C categorical-exemption narrative logged earlier in this batch (EO 10440, EO 10463): here the bypass is not a category of positions but a single named person, with the only stated justification being a one-line departmental recommendation. Worth watching whether individual-name civil-service bypass orders like this recur elsewhere in the corpus.

title: Directing Federal Agencies To Cooperate With State and Local Authorities in Preventing Pollution of the Atmosphere

flags.justification: A hortatory directive to federal agencies; explicitly preserves state and local primacy over air-pollution control rather than creating new federal authority.
No accountability mechanism is removed; the order creates no federal enforcement power at all.
A single, narrow cooperative-action directive.
'Such action as may be practicable and consistent with law' is a wholly undefined, aspirational standard with no enforcement mechanism, defined obligations, or reporting requirement attached.
No incentive structure at issue.
A standing policy directive; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No oversight channel is narrowed; if anything, the order affirmatively defers to state and local law.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects; as a purely hortatory order with no enforcement teeth, its practical effect was limited.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: An early (pre-EPA, pre-Clean Air Act enforcement regime) federal environmental-policy statement, notable historically but structurally weak by design: it creates no enforcement mechanism and defers explicitly to state/local primacy per the 1955 Air Pollution Control Act's stated policy.

title: Suspension of Certain Provisions of Section 5762(a), Title 10, United States Code, Which Relate to Promotion of Officers of Supply Corps, Chaplain Corps, Civil Engineer Corps, and Medical Service Corps of the Navy

flags.justification: A narrow, statute-authorized suspension of a specific promotion-quota provision for named Navy officer corps, under explicit statutory suspension authority (10 U.S.C. 5785(b)).
No accountability mechanism removed; the underlying provision resumes automatically once its termination condition is met.
Single, narrow promotion-provision suspension.
The suspended provision and its scope are specifically and narrowly defined by statutory citation.
No incentive structure at issue.

The suspension runs 'until June 30 of the fiscal year following that in which the national emergency proclaimed by Proclamation No. 2914 of December 16, 1950, shall end' — tied to an external, objective event (the formal end of the emergency) rather than to open-ended presidential discretion, which is a structurally better design than a purely open-ended 'until terminated or revoked' clause; however, because Proclamation 2914 itself was not formally terminated for decades, this tether proved just as durable in practice as an open-ended clause would have been.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects beyond the emergency-duration question already captured under Flag 6.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Part of the Proclamation 2914 open-ended-duration pattern, but a structurally distinct variant worth separating from EO 10764's purely open-ended form: here the suspension is tied to the formal end of the proclaimed emergency (an objective, if ultimately very long-delayed, external event) rather than to unfettered presidential discretion to terminate. Issued in tandem with EO 10781 (same date, same statutory basis, different Title 10 section).

title: Suspension of Certain Provisions of Section 5764(a), Title 10, United States Code, Which Relate to Establishment of Zones for Promotion of Male Officers of the Navy

flags.justification: A narrow, statute-authorized suspension of a specific promotion-zone-establishment provision for Navy line officers, under explicit statutory suspension authority (10 U.S.C. 5785(b)).
No accountability mechanism removed; the underlying provision resumes automatically once its termination condition is met.
Single, narrow promotion-provision suspension.
The suspended provision and its scope are specifically and narrowly defined by statutory citation.
No incentive structure at issue.

Tied to the same Proclamation 2914 emergency-end condition as its companion order EO 10780, issued the same day — an objective-event tether rather than an open-ended presidential-discretion clause, though subject to the same practical durability concern given Proclamation 2914's decades-long formal continuation.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects beyond the emergency-duration question already captured under Flag 6.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Companion order to EO 10780 (same date, same statutory basis); part of the Proclamation 2914 open-ended-duration pattern, in its emergency-end-tethered variant rather than the fully open-ended form seen in EO 10764.

title: Amending Executive Order No. 10773 of July 1, 1958, Relating to Civil and Defense Mobilization

flags.justification: A purely nominal renaming amendment ('Defense and Civilian Mobilization' to 'Civil and Defense Mobilization'), made to conform to a subsequent Act of Congress (Aug. 26, 1958); no substantive authority is altered.
No accountability mechanism affected.
No bundling; a single renaming change.
No enforcement standard at issue.
No incentive structure.
A renaming amendment to a standing reorganization order; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive at issue.
No material second-order effects beyond the renaming itself.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Direct follow-up to EO 10773 (this batch, two months prior): a purely nominal rename of the newly consolidated defense/civil-mobilization office, driven by an intervening Act of Congress rather than any substantive reconsideration of the July 1958 consolidation itself.

title: Transferring Certain Functions From the Department of Defense to the National Aeronautics and Space Administration

flags.justification: Implements the newly-enacted National Aeronautics and Space Act of 1958 by transferring specific, named space-related projects (Project VANGUARD, and named ARPA/Air Force space activities) from DoD to the newly created NASA; a statute-driven, one-time functional transfer rather than a discretionary concentration of power.

No accountability mechanism removed; Sec. 2's budget transfers are tied to specific dollar figures or a Bureau of the Budget determination under an existing statutory procedure (section 202(b) of the Budget and Accounting Procedures Act).
A single, coherent functional-transfer action implementing one new statute.

Sec. 1(b) defers full specification of which particular ARPA/Air Force space projects are covered to 'one or more supplementary Executive orders hereafter issued' — an open-ended deferral of scope to future, unspecified orders.
No incentive structure at issue.
A one-time functional transfer establishing a new agency's initial capability; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No oversight channel narrowed.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.

A historically significant order establishing NASA's initial operational capability by transferring the nation's civilian space program's founding assets (Project VANGUARD and key ARPA/Air Force space activities) out of the Defense Department — a foundational moment in the civilian/military space-program divide that shaped decades of subsequent space policy.
No inter-agency conflict; a clean, statute-directed handoff between DoD and NASA with cooperative property/personnel-transfer procedures (Sec. 2(b)).
No exemption architecture.

notes: Historically foundational (establishes NASA's initial operational capability under the National Aeronautics and Space Act of 1958) but structurally clean and statute-driven; the only notable structural gap is Sec. 1(b)'s deferral of full project-scope specification to future orders.

title: Specification of Laws From Which Functions Authorized by Mutual Security Act of 1954, as Amended, Shall Be Exempt

flags.justification: Exempts foreign-aid contracting functions from nine separate procurement-integrity statutes (Sec. 1) and four more governing overseas purchases (Sec. 2), based on the President's own determination that doing so 'will further the purposes of the Mutual Security Act.'
No accountability mechanism is removed beyond the procurement-statute exemptions themselves; agencies remain subject to whatever internal controls they separately maintain.

Bundles exemptions from thirteen distinct procurement-related statutory provisions (small-business set-aside law, anti-deficiency-adjacent statute, competitive-bidding and negotiation-authorization provisions, advance-payment restrictions, contract-modification-consideration requirements, and Merchant Marine cargo-preference law, among others) into a single order.

'Will further the purposes of the Mutual Security Act' is an undefined, self-determined standard applied uniformly across all thirteen exempted statutes with no case-by-case justification required in the order itself.
No distinct incentive structure beyond the bundled exemptions already captured under Flag 3.
A standing procurement-exemption framework tied to an ongoing foreign-aid statute; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.
No court or congressional oversight channel is narrowed; the exemptions concern internal procurement-process statutes rather than external review mechanisms.

Sec. 3 permits cost-type, no-fee contracts with non-profit institutions to be amended or modified 'with or without consideration...irrespective of rights which may have accrued under the contract' — a broad grant of contract-modification flexibility that could affect the parties' bargained-for positions after the fact.
No material second-order effects beyond the bundled-exemption concentration already captured under Flag 3.
No inter-agency conflict.

The entire order is itself an exemption-architecture instrument: it exists solely to specify which generally-applicable procurement-integrity laws will not apply to Mutual Security Act functions, bundling thirteen distinct statutory exemptions under one self-determined 'furthers the purposes of the Act' standard.

notes: Supersedes EO 10519 (1954); one of the more concentrated procurement-law-exemption bundles found in this batch, though confined to foreign-aid contracting rather than domestic government-wide procurement.

title: Change in the Membership of the President's Council on Youth Fitness

flags.justification: A routine membership-composition amendment (adds Secretary of Commerce and the HHFA Administrator) to an existing advisory council; no operative authority is altered.
No accountability mechanism affected.
No bundling; single membership change.
No enforcement standard at issue.
No incentive structure.
Standing advisory council; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply to a membership amendment.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Fourth order in the EO 10673/10704/10740/10785 chain governing this advisory council's composition; purely a membership-roster amendment.

title: Transferring Functions of the Airways Modernization Board to the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Agency

flags.justification: Implements the newly-enacted Federal Aviation Act of 1958 by transferring the Airways Modernization Board's functions to the FAA Administrator; a statute-driven, one-time functional transfer establishing the FAA's initial capability.
No accountability mechanism removed; property/personnel transfer details are directed by the Bureau of the Budget Director under an existing statutory framework (Sec. 2).
A single, coherent functional-transfer action implementing one new statute.
The transfer is tied to a specific triggering event (the entry into office of the first FAA Administrator, Sec. 3) rather than an open-ended standard.
No incentive structure at issue.

Sec. 3 provides that the transferred functions 'may be performed by the Administrator until the effective date of the repeal of the Airways Modernization Act of 1957' — a clean, statute-tethered expiration point for the interim arrangement.
No oversight channel narrowed.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects beyond establishing FAA's initial operational capability, a foundational but structurally routine transfer.
No inter-agency conflict; a clean, statute-directed handoff from the Airways Modernization Board to the FAA.
No exemption architecture.

title: Transferring Jurisdiction Over Certain Lands From the Department of Agriculture to the Department of the Interior, for Use, Administration, or Exchange Under the Taylor Grazing Act and Other Statutes

flags.justification: A detailed, surveyed land-jurisdiction transfer between two federal departments, made upon the recommendation of the Secretary of Agriculture; no power concentration beyond the transfer itself.
No accountability mechanism affected; the transfer preserves 'valid existing rights' and continues an existing county revenue-sharing arrangement.

Bundles land-jurisdiction transfers across five states (California, Montana, New Mexico, Texas, and by extension any other affected state) and multiple distinct land-utilization projects into a single order.
Each transferred parcel is defined by precise survey descriptions and acreage figures.
No incentive structure at issue.

A one-time jurisdictional transfer; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply, though the proviso for lands still 'in process of acquisition' ties the transfer's effective date to an objective completion event.
No oversight channel affected.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects.
No inter-agency conflict; a clean, coordinated handoff between Agriculture and Interior with revenue-sharing continuity for affected counties preserved.
No exemption architecture beyond the standard existing-rights preservation clause.

notes: A positive detail: the order explicitly preserves the 25%-of-net-revenue payment to affected counties (in lieu of the otherwise-applicable Taylor Grazing Act rate), maintaining local-government revenue continuity through the jurisdictional transfer.

title: Suspension of Compliance With Certain Statutory Provisions Relating to Employment in Canal Zone

flags.justification: Suspends compliance with congressionally-enacted statutory employment provisions in two appropriations acts based on the President's own determination that suspension is 'in the public interest' — a self-judged standard for setting aside otherwise-applicable statutory text.

The suspension operates under specific statutory authority Congress itself included in the appropriations acts, meaning Congress built in this flexibility rather than having it imposed around a congressional enactment.
A single, narrow suspension of specified employment-related statutory provisions.
'Deeming such course to be in the public interest' is an undefined, self-certified standard with no criteria specified for what qualifies.
No incentive structure beyond the underlying compensation questions, which are explicitly carved out and preserved by the order's proviso.

No expiration date is specified for the suspension itself beyond the underlying appropriations acts' own duration; the suspension persists for as long as the acts remain in effect with no separate review point.
No court or congressional oversight channel is narrowed; Congress retains its ordinary power to amend or reassert the suspended provisions in future appropriations.
No third-party/contractor incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects beyond the compensation-related carve-out already addressed by the proviso.
No inter-agency conflict.
The proviso itself creates a carved-out exception preserving compensation-amount limits even while suspending the broader employment provisions — a bounded exemption-within-an-exemption structure.

notes: Annual-recurrence template: essentially identical in structure and phrasing to EO 10726 (this batch, Aug. 1957), reissued each fiscal year to track the relevant appropriations acts (here, the FY1959 Commerce and Defense appropriation acts).

title: Authorizing Agencies of the Government To Exercise Certain Contracting Authority in Connection With National-Defense Functions and Prescribing Regulations Governing the Exercise of Such Authority

flags.justification: Authorizes the Department of Defense, and independently extends the same authority to ten additional civilian agencies (Part II), to enter into and modify contracts 'without regard to the provisions of law relating to the making, performance, amendment, or modification of contracts' whenever, 'in the judgment of the Secretary of Defense' (or equivalent agency head), 'the national defense will be facilitated thereby' — a sweeping, self-judged procurement-law bypass spanning eleven separate federal agencies.

Para. 6's congressional-reporting requirement (below) is itself qualified by a self-determined national-security nondisclosure exception, and Para. 5's public-inspection requirement carries the identical self-determined carve-out — the order's own transparency mechanisms can be turned off by the same officials whose actions they are meant to check.

Bundles an extraordinarily broad range of procurement-law bypasses (contract formation, performance, amendment, modification, advance payments, claim settlement, accrued-obligation releases) into a single authorization, extended identically across eleven agencies with substantively different missions (defense, but also Treasury, Interior, Agriculture, Commerce, AEC, GSA, the newly-renamed Office of Civil and Defense Mobilization, NASA, FAA, TVA, and GPO).

'In the judgment of the Secretary of Defense...the national defense will be facilitated thereby' is an undefined, self-certified standard with no external criteria specified for what qualifies as facilitating the national defense.

No perverse incentive structure beyond the procurement-bypass mechanism already captured under Flags 1 and 3; Para. 10's anti-kickback contractor warranty and Para. 7's non-discrimination clause are affirmative safeguards built into every contract made under this authority.

A standing procurement-authorization framework tied to an underlying statute and the ongoing Proclamation 2914 emergency; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply to the authorization mechanism itself, though individual contracts remain subject to their own terms.

Invokes 'the existing national emergency declared by Proclamation No. 2914 of December 16, 1950' as continuing legal predicate nearly eight years after the Korean War armistice, with no textual acknowledgment that the underlying conflict ended in 1953 — consistent with the Proclamation 2914 stale-invocation pattern tracked elsewhere in this batch (EO 10764, EO 10780, EO 10781).

Para. 10's mandatory anti-kickback/no-contingent-fee warranty clause and Para. 7's mandatory non-discrimination clause are affirmative, defined obligations imposed on every contractor benefiting from this bypass authority — a genuine, textually-specific check on the private parties who gain from the relaxed procurement rules.

Despite its breadth, this order contains some of the more substantial countervailing safeguards found in this batch's 'without regard to law' bypass instances: a $50,000 threshold requiring Assistant-Secretary-or-above approval (Para. 2); a mandatory annual report to Congress itemizing contractor name, cost, description, and justification for actions over $50,000 (Para. 6); mandatory Comptroller General audit-access clauses in every contract for three years post-payment (Para. 11); and an explicit list of actions the order does NOT authorize, including cost-plus-percentage-of-cost contracting, bond waivers, and contract-price increases above the lowest rejected bid (Para. 12) — a meaningfully more checked bypass than, for example, the ministerial-approval-removal template's typically unqualified 'without approval of the President' grants.

Part II's extension to ten additional agencies is structured as each agency head exercising the authority 'independently,' with a uniformity-encouragement provision (Para. 22) rather than creating direct overlap between agencies.

The entire order is an exemption-architecture instrument at an unusually large scale: eleven agencies are exempted from the government's ordinary contract-formation and modification laws, bounded only by the specific, enumerated carve-outs in Para. 12 and the reporting/audit mechanisms in Paras. 5, 6, and 11.

notes: One of the most comprehensive procurement-law-bypass orders found in this batch (eleven agencies, broad subject-matter scope, self-judged 'national defense' trigger, and yet another stale invocation of the December 1950 Proclamation 2914 nearly eight years after the Korean War armistice) — but also one of the better-checked: mandatory congressional reporting, mandatory GAO audit access, a dollar-threshold approval floor, and an explicit list of prohibited actions are real, textually-specific safeguards largely absent from this batch's other major bypass instances (compare EO 10713's Ryukyu Islands order or the ministerial-approval-removal template, neither of which carries any comparable reporting or audit mechanism). The self-determined national-security exception to the reporting and public-inspection requirements (Flag 2) is the one place where those safeguards could themselves be switched off by the same officials being checked.

No third-party incentive structure at issue.
No material second-order effects from this narrow update.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture created by this specific amendment.

No accountability mechanism removed; budget transfers are tied to a specific dollar figure (Sec. 4).
A single, coherent functional-transfer action.

No incentive structure at issue.
A one-time functional transfer; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.

title: Establishing the Canal Zone Merit System and Prescribing Regulations Relating to Conditions of Employment in the Canal Zone

flags.justification: Delegates to the Secretary of the Army broad authority to exclude any employee or position from the merit-system act entirely (Sec. 2(a)(1)), extend U.S.-citizen-equivalent rights to non-citizens, and promulgate implementing regulations — a significant concentration of discretion in one Cabinet officer over an entire non-domestic civilian workforce.

The Secretary of the Army's exclusion authority (Sec. 2(a)(1)) is subject only to a consultation requirement (Sec. 2(d): Navy, Air Force, Panama Canal Company, Canal Zone Government, CSC, others) and the Board of Appeals' review of individual employee appeals (Sec. 4) — no independent body reviews the exclusion determinations themselves.
A single, coherent employment-system-establishment function serving one statutory purpose.

Sec. 2(c)(3)'s standard for excluding employees/positions ('only for reasons for which exclusions or exceptions are made from the competitive civil service') is somewhat self-referential, and Sec. 2(c)(2)'s security-position criteria ('those which require the use of United States citizens to insure continuity and capability of operation... by the United States Government') are broadly worded.
No incentive structure beyond the merit-system framework itself.

A standing employment-system framework tied to an ongoing statute; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply, though Sec. 5(b) sets a firm implementation deadline (no later than January 19, 1959).

No court or congressional oversight channel is narrowed; Sec. 3(c) requires the Civil Service Commission to periodically review the merit system's conformity and report findings to the Secretary of the Army and the Special Assistant to the President for Personnel Management.
No private third-party incentive structure at issue.

Sec. 2(c)(2) explicitly incorporates by reference, without independent re-scrutiny, both EO 10501 (the classified-information order) and EO 10450 ('Security Requirements for Government Employment' — this stream's highest-scoring order at 65%, which gives agency heads unreviewable national-security removal authority and bars the Loyalty Review Board from hearing appeals): 'nothing in this order shall be deemed to modify or supersede any provision of either' order. This means the Canal Zone Merit System's citizen-only security-position designations are anchored to the same structurally troubled loyalty/security-removal regime already flagged in this batch, inherited wholesale into a new employment context.
No inter-agency conflict; Sec. 2(d)'s consultation requirement affirmatively coordinates across the several departments with Canal Zone employees.
Sec. 2(a)(1)'s exclusion authority is itself a standing exemption mechanism, allowing the Secretary of the Army to remove any employee or position from the merit system's coverage.

notes: Diff-aware: Sec. 4's Canal Zone Board of Appeals is a genuinely balanced five-member body (one CSC-nominated, two employee-representative-consulted, two Secretary-of-the-Army-selected members, majority-vote decisions) — a meaningfully more balanced appeals structure than several other gatekeeping bodies logged elsewhere in this batch that lack any employee-side representation. However, Sec. 2(c)(2)'s explicit non-modification clause for EO 10450 and EO 10501 means this new merit system's security-related exclusions rest on the same structurally flagged loyalty-security regime already identified as this batch's highest-scoring (most concerning) order.

flags.justification: Delegates to the Civil Service Commission the authority to designate any department, department subdivision, or employee as 'excepted from the Act or any provision of the Act' other than five specified core sections (Sec. 4(a)) — a broad, government-wide exemption-granting power.

Sec. 5(a)-(b) requires the Commission to consult the Special Assistant to the President for Personnel Management, but that same Special Assistant may 'exclude any specific matter or matters from the operation' of even that consultation requirement and 'terminate any exclusion' at will — verbatim the same standardless self-administered exclusion mechanism first flagged at EO 10729 (this batch), now reused in a second, distinct statutory context (employee training rather than personnel actions generally).
A single, coherent training-program-implementation function.
No criteria are specified for the Commission's exception-designation power under Sec. 4(a), nor for the Special Assistant's exclusion/inclusion decisions under Sec. 5(b).
No incentive structure beyond the exemption mechanism already captured under Flags 1-2.
A standing program-implementation framework tied to an ongoing statute; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.

The Sec. 5(b) self-administered exclusion mechanism is structurally identical to the ministerial-approval-removal template's escalated form first identified at EO 10729 — here confirming that the Special Assistant's standardless discretion to define the scope of CSC consultation requirements is a general-purpose device being reapplied across multiple statutory domains, not a one-off feature of the personnel-management order alone.

No private third-party incentive structure at issue, though Sec. 2(g) requires department heads to screen outside training-related gifts/awards for whether they would 'place or tend to place the recipient under any improper obligation to the grantor' — an affirmative conflict-of-interest safeguard.

Confirms that the EO 10729 exclusion mechanism is a reusable institutional template rather than a single-context device — worth watching for further reuse of the identical 'exclude any specific matter... terminate any exclusion' language across other Special-Assistant-touching orders later in the corpus.
No inter-agency conflict; Sec. 2(f) affirmatively encourages inter-departmental training cooperation.
Sec. 4(a)'s CSC exception-designation power is itself a standing, government-wide exemption architecture for the training-act framework.

notes: Significant cross-reference finding for notable-patterns-log.md: Sec. 5's self-administered exclusion mechanism is verbatim identical to EO 10729's Special Assistant to the President for Personnel Management provision, now applied to the Government Employees Training Act rather than personnel actions generally — confirming the mechanism is a reusable template being deliberately extended to new statutory domains, not confined to the office's original personnel-management context.

title: Amending the Selective Service Regulations

title: Further Providing for the Administration of Foreign-Aid Functions

Notably, Sec. 1(j) explicitly EXCLUDES from the delegation of section 403 functions 'the function of...determining any provision of law to be disregarded to achieve the purposes of that section' — the broadest law-disregarding power is expressly retained by the President rather than delegated, a deliberate and positive carve-out.

title: Providing for the Carrying Out of Certain Provisions of the Atomic Energy Act of 1954, as Amended, Relating to International Cooperation

flags.justification: Jointly delegates to the Secretary of Defense and the Atomic Energy Commission the authority to determine, without the President's approval, whether proposed nuclear-cooperation arrangements and Restricted Data communications pose an 'unreasonable risk to the common defense and security' — a consequential national-security determination delegated away from the President.

Sec. 2(b) requires disagreements between the Secretary of Defense and the AEC to be escalated to the President for decision, rather than defaulting to either party — a defined, meaningful check largely absent from other instances of this template.
A single, coherent nuclear-cooperation-determination function.

'Will promote and will not constitute an unreasonable risk to the common defense and security' is an undefined standard, though it is a dual-key determination (SecDef and AEC must agree) rather than a single officer's unilateral judgment.
No incentive structure beyond the joint-determination mechanism already captured under Flags 1-2.
A standing delegation for an ongoing statutory international-cooperation framework; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.

Sec. 2(a)'s 'without the approval, ratification, or other action of the President' language is a further instance of the ministerial-approval-removal template (this batch), here applied to nuclear-cooperation risk determinations — a genuinely consequential subject matter — but notably better-checked than most other instances: the authority is jointly held by two independent agencies (SecDef and AEC) rather than a single officer, and Sec. 2(b) requires unresolved disagreement to escalate to the President rather than defaulting to either agency's unilateral view.
No private third-party incentive structure at issue.

This is one of the more consequential-subject-matter instances of the ministerial-approval-removal template in this batch (nuclear-materials/technology cooperation risk determinations), comparable in stakes to the war-criminal-clemency thread (EO 10613, EO 10747) — but structurally the best-designed instance found so far, given the dual-key requirement and the disagreement-escalation-to-President clause.
Sec. 2(b)'s disagreement-escalation clause is itself an inter-agency-conflict-resolution mechanism, addressing directly the risk that a dual-key delegation could deadlock.
No additional exemption architecture beyond the delegation already captured under Flags 1 and 7.

notes: A structurally well-checked instance of the ministerial-approval-removal template: unlike most instances in this batch (single officer, no disagreement-resolution mechanism), this order requires SecDef and AEC to agree jointly and escalates any disagreement to the President — worth contrasting favorably against EO 10729's single-office, standardless-discretion escalation of the same template earlier in this batch. Sec. 3 also expressly clarifies that the underlying section 91c program-approval function itself is NOT delegated by this order, a further limiting feature.

title: Creating a Board of Inquiry To Report on a Labor Dispute Affecting the Steel Industry of the United States

flags.justification: A three-member fact-finding board under Taft-Hartley LMRA section 206, addressing the 1959 nationwide steel strike; the President's own finding that the strike 'will, if permitted to continue, imperil the national health and safety' is the statutorily-required predicate.

Unlike the RLA Emergency Board template's clean self-termination, the LMRA Board of Inquiry 'shall continue in existence to perform such other functions as may be required under such Act, until the Board is terminated by the President' — an indefinite continuation with no fixed end date.
A single, coherent fact-finding function, notwithstanding the underlying dispute's national economic significance.
The Board's powers and duties are defined by reference to Title II of the Labor-Management Relations Act rather than left open-ended.
No incentive structure at issue beyond the indefinite-continuation question already captured under Flags 2 and 6.
The Board's initial reporting deadline is fixed (October 16, 1959), but its subsequent existence is open-ended ('until the Board is terminated by the President') rather than self-terminating.

No court or congressional oversight channel is narrowed; the Board operates within the LMRA's own statutory framework for national-emergency labor disputes, which itself contemplates subsequent injunction proceedings in federal court.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.

Addresses the 1959 steel strike, one of the largest and most economically significant labor disputes of the postwar era; the Board's continuation pending further presidential termination reflects the LMRA's broader emergency-dispute machinery (injunction, cooling-off period, secret ballot) rather than an open-ended executive power grab.
No inter-agency conflict.
No exemption architecture.

notes: Sixth instance of the Taft-Hartley LMRA section 206 Board of Inquiry indefinite-continuation template already tracked in this batch (EO 10490, 10542, 10543, 10689, 10710), again contrasting with the RLA Emergency Board's clean self-termination — here applied to one of the postwar era's most significant labor disputes (the 1959 steel strike).

title: Extension of the Application of the Federal Aviation Act of 1958

flags.justification: Extends FAA Administrator authority extraterritorially to areas under U.S. jurisdiction abroad, but conditions any such action on prior consultation with, and an effective veto by, both the Secretary of State (on foreign-relations/treaty-conflict grounds) and the Secretary of Defense (on national-defense grounds).

title: Safeguarding Classified Information Within Industry

flags.justification: Establishes the government-wide Industrial Security Program, vesting each participating department head with authority to grant, deny, or revoke industrial personnel security clearances; Sec. 9 preserves a further, non-delegable authority for the department head to deny or revoke access 'if the security of the nation so requires' whenever he determines the ordinary procedures 'cannot be invoked consistently with the national security,' and that determination 'shall be conclusive.'

Sections 2-8 establish a genuinely substantial due-process regime (written statement of reasons, opportunity to reply under oath, personal appearance, counsel, cross-examination rights subject to defined exceptions) — but Sec. 9's conclusive, non-delegable override renders that entire regime bypassable whenever the department head himself determines it cannot be 'consistently' applied, with no external review of that determination.
A single, coherent industrial-security-clearance adjudication framework.

Sec. 2's clearance-grant standard ('clearly consistent with the national interest') and Sec. 9's override-trigger standard are both undefined and self-judged, though Sec. 7 usefully clarifies that adverse determinations are 'in no sense...a determination as to the loyalty of the applicant' — a meaningful, protective clarification distinguishing this framework from EO 10450's loyalty-removal apparatus.
No incentive structure at issue beyond the due-process/override tension already captured under Flags 1-2.
A standing clearance-adjudication framework; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.

Sec. 9 is, functionally, an oversight-preemption mechanism: it allows the very due-process protections Sections 3-6 establish to be set aside whenever the department head unilaterally and conclusively determines that invoking them would be inconsistent with national security — a self-administered override of the order's own most protective provisions.
No private third-party incentive structure at issue; the order protects, rather than incentivizes, industry-employed individuals subject to clearance determinations.

A foundational order establishing what became the long-running Industrial Security Program / Defense Industrial Security Clearance framework; the due-process regime here (Sections 2-8) is considerably more protective than EO 10450's domestic-employee security-removal apparatus (this stream's highest-scoring order, 65%) — written notice, cross-examination, counsel, and a written final decision are all present — making this a genuinely more careful design applied to a closely related subject (industrial personnel handling classified information) even though it shares the same conclusive-override escape valve.
No inter-agency conflict; Sec. 1(a) requires participating agencies to seek uniform, cooperative regulations 'so far as possible.'
Sec. 9's non-delegable, conclusive override is itself a standing exemption from the entire due-process structure the rest of the order establishes.

notes: Worth direct comparison with EO 10450 ('Security Requirements for Government Employment,' this stream's highest-scoring order, 65%, which bars the Loyalty Review Board from hearing appeals and folds unrelated personal-conduct criteria into national-security removals): EO 10865 is textually far more protective of individual due-process rights for an analogous population (industrial personnel handling classified information rather than direct federal employees) — real cross-examination rights, counsel, a written statement of reasons, and an explicit clarification (Sec. 7) that adverse findings are not loyalty determinations. But it shares the same fundamental structural feature as EO 10450: a conclusive, self-determined, non-delegable 'national security requires it' override (Sec. 9) that can set aside all of the preceding due-process machinery, with no external check on that determination. Logged as a major finding for notable-patterns-log.md.
