title: Administration of Mutual Security and Related Functions

flags.justification: Consolidates the entire EO 10575 foreign-aid delegation chain (superseding EO 10575, 10610, 10625, 10663, 10742, and parts of EO 10822 — all previously logged in this batch) into a single comprehensive order, centralizing foreign-aid-program administration primarily in the Secretary of State with defined carve-outs for Defense, Treasury, Commerce, and the Development Loan Fund.

Sec. 301's continuity provisions keep the International Cooperation Administration and its functions 'subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State,' and Sec. 110 explicitly reserves a substantial list of the most consequential functions (Senate-confirmed appointments, numerous findings/determinations/certifications) to the President rather than delegating them away.

One of the densest consolidation orders in this batch: reallocates functions across dozens of statutory sections spanning six agencies/officials (State, Defense, Treasury, Commerce, Development Loan Fund, USIA) plus detailed personnel, funding-allocation, and cost-sharing provisions, superseding five prior orders in a single instrument.

Sec. 109(c)'s 'without regard to existing laws governing obligation and expenditure' authority is confined to three specifically enumerated categories (efficiency/economy, personnel hardship mitigation, foreign tort-claim settlement) rather than an open-ended standard.
No incentive structure at issue beyond the consolidation density already captured under Flag 3.
A standing foreign-aid administration framework; a sunset expectation does not meaningfully apply.

No oversight channel narrowed; Sec. 110(4) explicitly and deliberately RETAINS with the President 'the function of...determining any provision of law to be disregarded' under section 403 of the Act — continuing the same positive non-delegation choice already noted at EO 10822 (this batch) through this larger consolidation.
No third-party incentive structure at issue.

A major consolidation event comparable in scale to EO 10480 (Defense Production Act consolidation) and EO 10773 (ODM/civil-defense consolidation) — this order becomes the operative foreign-aid-administration framework for the remainder of the Eisenhower administration and reflects the program's substantial maturation and growth since EO 10575's original 1954 design.

Sec. 102(b) and Sec. 106 include explicit inter-agency consultation and cost-sharing coordination requirements (SecDef must consult SecState on section 550 functions; cost-sharing functions are delegated to the several agency heads for their own personnel).
No additional exemption architecture beyond the bounded fiscal-flexibility categories already captured under Flag 4.

notes: Major standalone finding: the culmination of the EO 10575 foreign-aid-administration amendment chain tracked throughout this batch (EO 10610, 10625, 10663, 10742, 10822), consolidating all of it into a single order. Like EO 10480 and EO 10773 before it, this represents a significant concentration event in raw structural terms (Flag 3 CRITICAL), but — notably, and consistent with EO 10822's earlier design choice — the order carries forward rather than abandons the deliberate retention of the broadest law-disregarding power with the President (Sec. 110(4)), and bounds its 'without regard to law' fiscal-flexibility grant (Sec. 109(c)) to three specifically enumerated categories rather than leaving it open-ended. A consolidation that concentrates administrative authority while still declining to hand over its most consequential discretionary power.

title: Establishing the President's Committee on Migratory Labor

title: Amendment of Executive Order 6260 of August 28, 1933, as Amended

flags.justification: Adds a substantial new prohibition (Sec. 12): U.S. persons may not acquire, hold, or retain gold coin, certificates, or bullion situated OUTSIDE the United States without a Treasury-issued license; the Secretary of the Treasury is given open-ended licensing discretion ('subject to such other regulations as he may prescribe') to permit exceptions, with no further statutory or regulatory bound on that discretion beyond a defined transition date.

Tied to the continued invocation of Proclamation 2914 of December 16, 1950 (Korean War emergency) as still-live legal predicate for a brand-new substantive prohibition enacted more than ten years after the underlying proclamation, with no textual reassessment of whether the emergency persists. notes: Major standalone finding, paired with EO 10896 (this chunk): within the same six-week span, the Proclamation 2914 authority chain is used once to narrow (EO 10896) and once to substantially broaden (this order, adding a new criminal-adjacent prohibition on gold holdings abroad) the underlying 1933 gold-regulation order — demonstrating that a decade-old, never-reassessed emergency proclamation can support new and more restrictive action just as readily as routine housekeeping, without any occasion for legislative or public reconsideration of whether the emergency itself still exists.

flags.justification: Restructures inter-agency responsibilities for watershed-protection and flood-control planning among Agriculture, Interior, the Army, and TVA; no single agency's discretion is expanded relative to the prior order.

A genuinely positive counter-example to inter-agency cannibalization: Sec. 4 requires each agency to affirmatively request the others' views on the feasibility of achieving overlapping objectives (irrigation, urban flood control, TVA-basin protection) BEFORE committing to a planning course, and Sec. 4(e) requires this consultation to occur before any commitment is made to local interests — an explicit ex ante duplication-avoidance mechanism.

notes: The Eisenhower administration's final executive order in this stream (Jan. 18, 1961, two days before Kennedy's inauguration): a well-designed, explicit ex ante inter-agency consultation requirement (Sec. 4) that affirmatively works to avoid the duplicative, cannibalizing infrastructure investment that other multi-agency frameworks in this batch left unaddressed — a fitting, low-weight, structurally clean bookend to Batch 1.
